name: Anonymous

>>130242450
REM is a really specific type of southen gothic rock at first that gradually evolves into a more mainstream but still distinct sound as their career goes on. It's also harder to recommend a specific starting album for REM and a lot of normalfags are put off with a recommendation like "Start with Murmur and listen to every album through New Adventures". They want a singular "THE" album to start with. Even Automatic despite being their biggest album commercially is a weird sort of one-off in their discography so recommending it as the starting point feels off.

Compare that to The Cure and The Smiths that comes from a similar background of 80s English alternative and have albums pretty much everyone points to as starting points that don't require listening to a large part of their discography. They're just more accessible in that way for most people. Which is kind of funny in retrospect remembering those few years in the early 90s where REM was one of the most popular bands in the world.

name: Anonymous

>>130243936
I guess my problem with this specific instance is the violent way it just... stopped.

The '80s saw the creation of a veritable universe of sound, crude and full of potential that the '90s promptly distilled into discrete forms, which were stretched and pushed to and beyond its boundaries and logical conclusion during the '00s. I don't think any other genre of popular music ever underwent such an expansive, quick evolution only to suddenly become stagnant. The early '10s gave some interesting example of a kind of reversal: Subgenres and styles melded back together in a manner analogue to the primordial soup of the '80s, except with a thoroughly contemporary musical language, but otherwise all we've gotten since has been a torrent of purposefully retrorevivalist, born-in-the-wrong-generation, uncreative tripe, or the ongoing, yawning echoes of what was already being perfected 15 years ago.

It's all just very sad, really.

No this is incorrect. Jazz went through the exact same evolutionary process of zenith into decline and irrelevance during the 20 year period of 1950-1970. Metal is simply too old of a genre to spawn fresh artists.

name: Anonymous

>>130244184
You say that like it's two mutually exclusive traits. All good music was innovative at some point. No matter how many, say, Darkthrone clones there are now, it was radical and innovative in 1993. The point is not that there should be more wildly experimental/avant-garde efforts, but that there are no new schools at all. What's the point, I say, of listening to this year's hottest new album when it sounds exactly like some average record released 30 years ago? When confronted with two identical musical acts, I'll gladly stick with the one that did it first. As for those who opine that "there can never be too much of X", I think only someone who doesn't really care about experiencing music at any level beyond a superficial one can be content with amassing hundreds of nearly identical releases and listen to every one of them as if they're all equally good. It doesn't make sense to me. One would have to think very little of the artistic experience.

Yes but the era in jazz music that is known as being the most innovative and creative didn't come about until much later. Heavy metal in it's infant stage began in the late 1960s, and we know that because articles from the time period referenced this new heavier genre of rock by calling it metal.

The classic metal period that produced the most popular metal albums lasted for around 20 years, from the early 1980s to the late 90s or early 2000s, mirrors the jazz zenith period in longevity.

>>130244216
>All good music was innovative at some point.
I disagree. That was the point of my post, it doesn't have to be new and original to be good, I like many of those "retrorevivalist" bands, what does it matter what year it was made in? If it's good, it's good.
>When confronted with two identical musical acts, I'll gladly stick with the one that did it first.
>amassing hundreds of nearly identical releases and listen to every one of them as if they're all equally good
I could never understand this mentality. First of all, they are never actually identical. They all have their little differences that are apparent if you actually enjoy and are familiar with the style. They're not equally good; there are better and worse ones, and some even surpass the original, i think comparing them and finding the best is part of the fun. You call it superficial, but to me your way is the one that seems superficial - if I understand correctly, you just listen to the same old albums over and over, just because they did it first, and assume that newer band are automatically inferior? I literally couldn't do that, I usually get bored of listening to the same thing really quickly, but I have no problem listening to dozens of similar bands in a style I like.
Perhaps the way we enjoy music is just fundamentally different in some way.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130244637
>I disagree
It's not up for debate. Everything that is not a direct copy was new once. If it's good and a copy, it's a copy of something good, and that something good was new once.
>Perhaps the way we enjoy music is just fundamentally different in some way.
Clearly. My whole point was pretty far removed from an argument about how one should go about enjoying things though. It was a quick summary of the development -and stagnation- of a genre, and it wasn't "incorrect".

>>130242422
it's certainly helpful to know the intervals for each of the notes in your chord shapes, like the usual Amaj shape is 1-5-1-3-5, Amaj7 is 1-5-7-3-5, etc etc. knowing this helps u be more aware of the fretboard, and helps with identifying the "important" notes in the chord.
and sometimes you need to spell out the chord, like if you find a chord that sounds good but you don't know it's name. or if youre writing a chord melody and say you need to play a EbMaj7 with a D on top, then knowing how EbMaj7 is "spelled" is very helpful in finding that voicing.
AUG talks about this in a bit more detail, ctrl+f KISSDIS in the pdf and you'll find it

>It was 2006. I had been waiting at 5150 studios for more than an hour when Eddie finally showed up. I hadn't seen him in a decade. He looked like he hadn't bathed in a week. He certainly hadn't changed his clothes in at least that long. He wasn't wearing a shirt. He had a giant overcoat and army pants, tattered and ripped at the cuffs, held up with a piece of rope. I'd never seen him so skinny in my life. He was missing a number of teeth and the ones he had left were black. His boots were so worn out he had gaffer's tape wrapped around them, and his big toe stuck out. He walked up to me, hunched over like a little old man, a cigarette in his mouth. He had a third of his tongue removed because of cancer and he spoke with a slight lisp.

>He may have lost a chunk of his tongue to cancer, but he was still smoking cigarettes. He claimed the cancer came from putting the guitar pick in his mouth while he used his fingers to play. He was living with a pathologist, who kept taking slices off his tongue, to check for cancer. He beat the cancer. He told me he cured himself by having pieces of his tongue liquefied and injected into his body. He also told me when he had his hip replacement, he stayed awake through the operation and helped the doctors drill the hole. What a fruitcake.

>We brought in producer Glen Ballard, who made Alanis Morissette's Jagged Little Pill, a total pro who really tried to make things happen. We had [new songs] ready within one week. It took three months for Ed to do the guitar parts to three songs and a couple of solos. The Eddie Van Halen I first met could have done that in an hour. When they finally laid down the rhythm-guitar tracks, that was all I needed to sing. I didn't need his guitar solos. As soon as they got that, I went in and knocked out my vocals on all three songs in two hours. Mike came in and we did all the backgrounds in another two hours. Eddie was still asleep. By the time he came down, we were finished. They spent the next three months doing Eddie's guitars.

>Our new manager, Irving Azoff, agreed to hold an intervention with Eddie. He brought a big, beefy security guard and met Al and me at 5150. Eddie walked in, carrying his wine bottle. Irving did all the talking. He told Eddie the [2004] tour was going to be difficult, that he needed to go away for a week or two, that we could postpone some dates if we needed. We all agreed Eddie needed to clean up.

>He smashed the bottle. "Fuck you," he said. "I will kill the first motherfucker that tries to take this bottle away from me. I left my family for this shit. You think I'm going to fucking do this for you guys?"

When he finally came out, he had this hairbrush, the kind with the fur bristles, twisted up in his hair, hanging down. He was soaking wet, covered in water, like he tried to take a bath in the airplane sink. He flopped down on the floor, fussing with the brush caught in his hair, and never went back to his seat, landed that way. Hospital-crazy.

>After one show, Mike and I stayed back, like we normally did, and showered. Ed didn't shower. He jumped into the limo right off the stage and went straight to the airplane. When Mike and I rolled up, laughing, joking, eating a couple of barbecue sandwiches we had ordered, Eddie was sitting there drinking his wine out of the bottle. He went off on us. "Don't ever fucking make me wait," he said. "Without me, you're nothing. You need me. You'll see. At the end of this tour, you guys will have nothing. You're going to have to call me if you ever want to tour again."

>Once in a while, I'd go over to his dressing room before the show and see how he was, and the times I did that it was usually great. He'd start playing, I'd start singing, jamming around, like old times. Other times, he'd start telling me crazy shit, like, "I pulled my own tooth — this thing was bugging me so I got a pair of pliers and pulled it out."

>I didn't think he could make it. I kept thinking each week would be the last. He was going to land in the hospital. He collapsed a couple of times. He told us one time that he had been hit by a car. He was lying down, and he was so fucked up, he couldn't get up. "I got hit by a car," he said. "You guys don't understand."

>His solo turned into a disaster. It used to be the highlight of every show. Now he would play nothing, just garbage. He would try to play "Eruption," one of his greatest pieces, and screw it up. He would just grab the whammy bar, hit the sustainer, and start making all this noise. The audience wasn't buying it, either.

>I didn't go near him onstage. No more Jimmy Page and Robert Plant. If he's over there, I'm over here. When he comes over here, I'm going over there. No bad vibes, just no vibes. It seemed to me that Ed was going through the motions, like he didn't care about his playing. He didn't care about the way he looked. He just went out there and took the money. He was embarrassing. Al, Mike and I did it from the heart. We played our asses off every night. Ed went out there and jerked off.

>Irving took me aside. "When this show's over," he told me, "I'm getting you in a limo, and we're getting out of here." My plane was waiting to take me home. It was the worst show we'd ever done in our lives. Eddie played so bad. He smashed his favorite guitar to pieces. Sprayed shrapnel into the crowd. He got on the microphone, crying. "You don't understand," he said. "You people pay my rent. I love you people."

I'm being genuine here, if you can point out some Angine de Poitrine zones where the microtonal elements are more than a device within a generally 12TET composition then I'll admit I'm wrong. From the set I watched on youtube it never ventured away from that, and in that case it's no different from Nick Reinhart putting on a wacky pitch shifter
People like Harry Partch used microtonality in a way that attempted to break free from 12TET compositions, that's just an extreme example, but it provides a reference to point out that most bands dabbling in microtonality use it as just an effect to decorate 12TET. There's nothing wrong with that, it can be sick, I just wouldn't call it new or innovating. Especially in this case since it's just a matter of tools, using a microtonal guitar instead of pedals

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130219630
My top tip would be don't make the same mistake I did and watch too many YT tutorials before actually using your DAW. The first few months will probably be spent figuring out the DAW, your preferred workflow and what works for sound selection and composition. For me, this part was very frustrating and daunting and made it hard to want to open the DAW on some days (sometimes for weeks on end), and I tried to justify it by watching YT tutorials so that I was still 'learning something', but all I needed to do was spend more time in the DAW experimenting and trying my best to make what I could. It's really obvious in hindsight, but it's easy to get overwhelmed by the amount of stuff you have to learn and fall into these kinds of procrastination traps. Pic unrelated.

>>130233069

Cynical outlook I know, but from my point of view it's a zero-sum game when you think about it, there is a finite number of people willing to spend money on music, and each person in that group has a finite amount of money they're willing to spend, without letting it eat into their other outgoings.

That means any artist who supports you, without knowing if you want to charge money for your music, is potentially taking money out of their own potential earnings. It's why you see so many collaborations now, because that works as profit-sharing which is preferable to no profit at all, and it's also why we keep seeing so much drama among rappers, who would much prefer to take all profit for themselves and only collaborate in circumstances where their combined publicity will increase total combined profit above what they would gain separately.

name: Anonymous

>>130235653
It depends, there’s a lot going into these commercial products, especially at small scales. Take elektron gear. It’s really well built, and well designed and gets meaningful software updates for years. They employ a lot of great people, and they need their salaries paid.

Modular gear is super expensive but it’s some guy programming a completely novel oscillator on an FPGA board with plenty of control and I/O that a few hundred people will use.


Your computer keyboard with some midi i/o and an x0x sequencer that took you a month to make with an arduino has totally different constraints and you’ve ignored your labour costs.


That said, this sort of thing is great for getting things to behave exactly how you need, and modern AI makes the labour costs much smaller.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130200102
Get used to doing baby-talk humming in your head silently (or even out loud at the beginning). You know like this but in pitch do do do dee dum do la do but according to the song you're trying to absorb. If you already know the song because you've heard it a bunch of times already, you'll pick up that song much easier. But if it's new, it's clean-sheet territory so it helps to listen to it being played several times. If possible you can use a slow-downer program to slow it down without changing pitch. Amazing SlowDowner from Roni Music will allow this. Transcribe from seven-string music will also allow this as well as both do looping and you can slow the tempo down without changing pitch. Then it becomes learning one note at a time or chord at a time. Advanced musicians look at the key signature and are generally able to figure out what chords are in the progression typically as well as what scales go with those chords. This isn't bulletproof but it allows musicians to skim a bit more. They have a lot of experience so they can figure a lot of this out on the fly. Us newbs can't yet.

For you though I'd look into synthesiagame.com for their Synthesia software. The video on their site isn't that good at really explaining what the program can do. That's where YouTube comes in. Try this e-leader guy, he's super chill and aimed at children basically so it's easy. Learn what the program can actually do. How you can slow stuff down and enable waiting mode. This all allows better learning retention. But basically it's all memorization. If you can't remember stuff, you'll have problems.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OWBTihldIm8

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130201639
Oh and the more notational reading you do every day (even 5 to 10 mins/day) will greatly help you to learn songs because you're actually reading notes. Not just memorizing sounds. Think of this as learning how to read a book with words in them. You first started out learning how to print the alphabet. Then you started to sound out words. Then you learned better reading and better writing and better speaking of these words because your vocabulary grew with practice and repetition. It took time and effort and daily practice. Music is a language of sound and needs to be treated just as you would learning a different language. The more you use it, the better. The more retention and comprehension you'll have. Eventually you can express your own thoughts and ideas and not just parrot others learning their songs. But that comes down to exposure, practice and listening to artists/musicians in your favorite genre(s) of music.

The killer thing is that there are just 12 notes in Western music. Just assembled in different patterns over different time periods. Just like a spoken sentence. In theory, it should be much easier to learn music with only 12 notes versus the English language using 26 letters. Deceptively easy eh? NOT so much as you know...

Oh and in the beginning say the names of the notes you play out loud. Even if just random notes. Build your note recognition and keyboard layout. Say those notes in pitch if possible. It can help you build your singing voice too and help memorization. At first you'll suck. But eventually you won't.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130201919
>The killer thing is that there are just 12 notes in Western music.
I mean this is still very hard, not learning the 12 notes but i mean everything else the chords and the techniques that go along them has an insane almost unlimited skill ceiling.

>>130205676
bowing on one string isn't that hard, i followed this technique where you deliberately over-press your bow to one string and slowly decrease the pressure to the point you no longer hear the squeaking and that's when you slowly get used to it. also the guessing pitch part is also part of the fun because it develops both your ear and finger memory.
coming from a guitar's background, it couldn't be helped that i wanted to shred on that too and of course it turned out badly but it's still a fun phase to make your bowing smoother, especially when you move to other strings.
i still think having some guitar experience will make a good base to move to violin, you still need to adjust that it's tuned in fifth compared to fourth though. but the good part of instruments tuned in fifth, you can play like C-D-E-G-A-B with the same set of fingers, same position but just in different strings so that's a good exercise as well.
I wish I had more time screwing around with my violin but i'm always too tired after work.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130215083
you don't learn the exact numbering, you learn it by deciding which leading voice the chord should sound.
like for example if you want the E note to sound more pronounce in your C major chord, then you place the E as the highest note in your fingering then the rest will follow (which means G-C-E).

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130215083
>Is there a formula like the first inversion is the fifth relative minor or something?
Inversions of a triad are just different vertical arrangements of the same notes -- they're not going to be become something else like the 'fifth relative minor.'
For example, a simple major triad in root position has the root note on the bottom, 3rd in the middle, and 5th on top. First inversion is the 3rd on the bottom, 5th in the middle, and roof on top.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130243846
First off, I feel like you should probably write this in 5/8, it would make much more sense in term of the drums and feel of the piece.

Secondly, you generally want your rhythms to be written in a way where it's obvious where the beats are. Looking at your sheet music at a first glance, it's not *immediately* obvious to me where the third beat lands compared to the chords on bar 53 or 54.

To solve this on bar 53 for instance, you should reverse the second half note and eight note, so that it's half note and tied to an eight note, and then another eight note tied to a half. That way everything would be more readable, and make it much easier to say "okay, it's ONE and two and three AND four and five and"

bar 54, avoid using dotted 8th notes if you're not writing a 16th right after, that's also confusing and it "hides" the beat

>>130244008
You really should know how to read rhythm even if you're not interested in reading notation overall

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130244208
Thank you. That makes sense, even more when I look at the latter parts like the one in the screenshot. Using 5/8 would basically halve it and let each little phrase be its own bar.
I hate Guitar Pro 8 for not having a feature which would automatically do this. Now I get to redo the whole score.

>>130244008
I'm also at the very early stage of learning, but the book I'm going through teaches via notation/sheet music. I don't even like or plan to play classical music, but I guess it won't hurt knowing the basics.

I also play guitar and I enjoy transcribing my favourite songs, so I can play along to the backing tracks I create for them. Sometimes I get into these situations which are way above my head. For example, I currently only "know" how to play 3 scales and C minor isn't one of them. This song is in C minor, so even though I transcribed this, I couldn't technically play this off the sheet music, because the flats aren't ingrained into my brain yet.

com:
I guessed by the album cover that you would be a sorta mathy metalcore but the comments itt about your bad singing made me curious. Your album was pretty hard to find with my youtube premium account, so I guess you're not using a distributor(?) May be selling yourself short on reach.
You obviously aren't mathy metalcore, it's navel gazing sadboy indie- your band logo doesn't convey this at all, and might be holding you back from new fans. It's not my type of music, but I'm glad that middle-of-the-road Weezer/Pavement/Dino Jr. shit is coming back around with young people, because that's what keeps venues open for us crusties and grinders and acid techno ravers to book during indie rock's off nights.
Your voice is unique enough, in a good way, to get attention from indieheads, but you *do* need a few professional lessons if you wanna get into a seriously higher tier of booking. It's a fine voice for this kinda stuff, but even just a local church choir director or someone can teach you how to dial in your pitch just a tad more.
Your voice really should be lower in the mix, because this is a rock album that verges on having some pretty big arena rock moments- not this Rhianna pop voice-up-front shit where I'm often not even able to focus on listening to the instrumentals. The drums should be brought forward a good bit, the guitar should be slightly louder, your vocals need to be dipped somewhere 800-100hz, and brought down. Also the vocals are way too dry- I can hear the reverb tail during drops so I know there's some dampness there, but I feel like the glue reverb and glue delay sends are down too much, and the whole thing needs to be wetter and maybe even a send to a slight bitcrusher or similar for a crunch that will help mix in better.
The electric "rock guitar" tone could use a little more high-mids.
Overall- I haven't turned it off yet, and I think this would make a great demo to send out back in the old days. Needs work.

com: The global recession back in 2008 made record companies clamp down on what got into the mainstream, and it was basically a race to the bottom in terms of talent while trying to promote individual artists instead of bands because they're easier to keep on a leash. The coincided with poptimism which was basically an attempt to lower critical standards so these same easy to control artists would receive high critical praise so normalfaggots felt like what they were listening to was "art". The end result was a musical environment with a few individual artists being huge for pretty much the entire decade while putting out mediocre forgettable music that was praised as being earth-shattering and incredible. The indie scene was also slowly eaten away until by the end of the decade there wasn't a coherent scene anymore so much as just randos putting out whatever while getting pretty much no attention. Things stopped bubbling up from the underground and everything got frozen in time.

>>130043292
Driver 23/The Atlas Moth is a 2-part documentary released in 1999 and 2001 about the failed Minneapolis band Dark Horse, and the process behind the creation of their 2000 album 'Guts Before Glory.' Lead singer Dan Cleveland recorded and mixed the entire album in at his house while taking psychiatric drugs for severe OCD and working a day-job as a delivery driver (#23) for an office supply company.

Among other issues, Cleveland got divorced during the production process, and also went bankrupt, along with the band's bass player Sean Cassidy (who died of a heart attack in 2004). Here's a track from the album for anyone curious:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Kk7Os-wBRko

>Country is fucked/dying
>Music is shit
>Three lads with absolutely zero life prospects form band to shag women
>Ask owner of fashionable shop to manage the band
>Somehow recruit one of the most unique characterful people as singer (who also had zero prospects)
>Make music just for the fuck of it
>Its starts an entire new music scene
>It totally revolutionises music
>It entirely reshapes the fabric of society
>Split up within a year and a half

name: Anonymous

>>130071127
You might, they intentionally showcase some of the most fried or obnoxious fans just because it's funny, so that could be entertaining. Directed by Todd Phillips so that's kind of interesting too.
At the very least, it gives a better perspective on the band than what the general public tends to assume because of the scene/fans. They're really just some dorky new england goofballs and nothing like the white dreaded hacky sack patchwork hippies that follow them.
Also if you're just interested in music history, they were one of the biggest touring acts of the 90s no question, this fact often seems downplayed because of how hated by the media they were, but they were a straight up cultural phenomenon in the 90s. Worth getting some exposure to that world for the sake of music culture/history if nothing else. That's the main reason I like it anyway

com: This is a very comprehensive six-part miniseries each episode about an hour long but is very well made and breaks down each era of the band into reasonable chunks. It's really good if you're curious about the whole Dead phenomena but also if you are a head and haven't seen it it contains some of the last filmed interviews with all the members/crew/orbiters who have pretty much all died by now except for the drummers. It came out in 2016 so I think it was filmed around 2015-15. Anyways its pretty trippy at times and sad at others but even if you hate the Dead it's interesting to watch just for the quality doc filmmaking. It explains how the whole cult around the band came to grow and grow and what it was like back in those boomer fantasy days

I'd like to present "5 Days: A Zenumentary". Pretty good coverage of an event that was part of the American rave scene in the mid-late 90s. Helps to serve as a reminder of how much variety there was in electronic dance music in that era...electro-influenced funky breaks were as big in the American scene as house or trance. Also ignore the first 5 minutes of the promoter pretending to be retarded.

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130043292
God Said Give 'Em Drum Machines covers some of the same ground as High Tech Soul, except unlike HTS, this one is actually good. Less random opining on the scene, and more hard history and coverage of internal conflict. It's good enough that I wish it was longer and made room to include more Detroit Techno artists.

>>130232246
What's your favourite, anon? Friendly Card is in my opinion the most cohesive, it's also the most orchestral out of all of them, which makes sense considering the medieval theme, but there's enough funk and jazz grooves to fuel the gambling aspect too. I like how you can go from something like TOAFC Part 1 which is basically a medieval fable, to Snake Eyes, then Time, one of APP's best songs, which is also entirely different because it's slow and contemplative, and then you have the much needed instrumental break of The Gold Bug before capping off the rest of the TOAFC suite. Wonderfully paced album if you ask me. Also, is it me, or is there a section in May Be A Price To Pay that sounds a lot like Another Brick In The Wall Part 2? I know Alan Parsons engineered on DSOTM, but is that an intentional nod, or had Pink Floyd cracked the sound of rebellion at a time to a point where a "marching" sort of feel with a similar enough guitar riff was just a coincidence?

>>130217265
This album would've been legit total kino if the production was actually good. Andy Johns nearly did it, which would've sounded great, but then they decided against it because of his coke habit. Problem is, Dennis MacKay who got the job was drunk for nearly all the sessions and completely mailed it in. Subsequently you have a piss weak drum sound and Bonnet's vocals are strained because he was singing at the absolute top of his range - a good producer would've brought it down half a step to make his vocals more comfortable.

The second album with Steve Vai has great production and way better vocals thanks to Eddie Kramer but then material isn't neatly as strong as the debut.

com:

i don't really mind the poor production that much. it definitely detracts from the album, but not by a lot and i think the material across the board more than makes up for it.

com: I've become a bit disillusioned with shoegaze over the past 3-4 years. The new wave of bands is just not really blowing me away. Most of them are pretty boring or just outright alt-Metal acts disguising themselves as shoegaze. In fact my home state seems to be produced new bands at a nice rate that get some nice nationwide attention, but I've seen them all live and they all kinda suck in terms of presentation and sonic weight. The hands are changing and the people at the ground floor making this scene a big deal don't seem too bothered in the music being pretty same-y. Probably just me.

16 years ago I was a 20 year old guitarist.
i would play page, malakian, dylan, simon, frusciante, etc, etc, etc. alone in my bedroom along with their albums on my mom's 40 year old hohner.
one day i was watching the news and nbc was having a contest for tickets to a Heart concert.
all you had to do was text the station and "be texter #33".
i text-bombed the station because i wasted to see wilson preform live.
i won.
not only did I win, i won ALL the tickets.
the station evidently planned to give anyone who texted in concert tickets, not just #33.
i was the only person sitting in my Zoso patched carhart farm coat front row at Brian Boitano's Skating Spectacular.
i can't find the aired program.
but i was there.
and it was great.

His actual music wasn't anything special all things considered. The bigger contribution was him being the original counter culture, anti-aurhority figure that paved the way for Punk Rock to break out despite very obviously not being part of the Punk Rock genre himself. His darker lyrics and appealing to social outcasts were influential inspiration for Metal, Punk, and more. So his music wasn't really that big of a deal, but Johnny Cash himself had one helluva influence on music.

Jello was still reacting against hippy culture going full-on yuppie at that point. Punks et. al who shared his mindset just associated yoga and organic food with the commodification of what was supposed to be a rebellious, transformative movement.
There's a sorta similar sentiment now with the queer community rejecting corporate sponsored events that are ostensibly "pro queer" but just look like giant commercials. Thats why Whales sing like they sing and their sounds travel long distances. Anyhow...

Ocean floor music however has to be more spooky, the music must reflect the visual image you are imagining when you imagine a biome like a little diorama in your mind.

>>130232249
I don't know that I can say much about Dean as a singer or a man or whatever, but I will say that his version of Gentle On My Mind is one of the more interesting ones.
Compared to, say, Glen Campbell's or Roger Miller's versions that are more melodic, Dean's delivery is very percussive which makes for a different kind of groove in the song. He was known for that kind of delivery, lagging slightly behind the beat in a sort of boozy manner, but Gentle On My Mind really highlights it.
You can hear his influence in singers like Willie Nelson too. You don't have Crazy without Dean.
I'd say he was an archetype that I sorely miss today.

com:
>>130232591
>Do you need animal products to survive?
depends on one's circumstances. I personally don't, but some people have monetary, otherwise practical, and even biological complications which disallow a fully vegan diet.
>So since you don't need to eat meat to survive, i.e. out of necessity, you are eating meating for pleasure
I'm not saying this is true of most meat-eaters, but this is a false dichotomy or an over-simplification. even if one could be a full-fledged vegan, there are hypothetically - and in reality - considerations beyond pure pleasure that would make someone choose to eat some portion of meat products.
> I think we can both agree killing for pleasure is a moral and ethical failure
I'm not so sure it is. the question is what the basis of morality is and whether we have as humans a higher privilege than other animals or something else which would not ethically contradict killing animals for food (which is why I asked the basis for morality). I'm not a robot, so I have my own "prejudices" about these things, but I'm skeptical of objective morality in general. and just saying "surely we can agree to this," when veganism/killing non-humans for food is a very contentious issue is not something I would grant on its face. vegans normally (have to) default to a sort of consequentialism, but it always fails imo when you get to the bottom of it. though I can appreciate the sentiment (at least to some extent).

name: Anonymous
com:
>>130214403
wrote for pitchfork in the way early days, quit in like 01 or 02 or something, did other music writing after
He made a series of videos starting in maybe 2011 or 12? Where he just talked about music and shit on writers, lots of hate towards pitchy and the way music publications work. It's called Shallow Rewards if you wanna look it up, he deleted them forever ago but they always get re-upped by somebody.
He's definitely very opinionated and has some annoying takes, but his videos were interesting because he just was a person actively engaged with the more hip music world back then and gave his perspective as such, as opposed to just hearing about all that stuff online through the lens of publications. He's def a bit snobby but desu he's self aware and critical about snobby hipster shit, and seems like a genuine music loving dork instead of some phoney indie trender. Take everything he says with a grain of salt but his videos are good and desu there's nothing else that really talks about music that way, worth checking out
